q: Mendel selected garden pea for his experiments mainly because it:
opts: Shows many clear contrasting traits and permits controlled pollination
Has an exceptionally long generation time
Produces only one seed per generation
exp: Pea plants have distinct contrasting characters, a short generation time and can be selfed or cross-pollinated.

q: The number of pairs of contrasting characters studied by Mendel was:
exp: Mendel studied seven pairs of contrasting traits in pea.

q: A true-breeding organism is one that:
opts: Produces offspring resembling the parent for a trait after repeated selfing
exp: True-breeding lines show stable trait inheritance after continuous self-pollination.

q: In a monohybrid cross TT × tt, all F1 offspring are:
exp: Each offspring receives T from one parent and t from the other.

q: Selfing the F1 of TT × tt gives an F2 phenotypic ratio of:
exp: Complete dominance produces three dominant phenotypes for every one recessive phenotype.

q: The F2 genotypic ratio in a monohybrid cross is:
exp: Segregation of T and t from Tt parents gives TT:Tt:tt = 1:2:1.

q: A test cross is performed between an individual of unknown dominant genotype and:
exp: The recessive tester reveals the gametes produced by the unknown genotype.

q: A tall pea plant gives tall and dwarf progeny in a test cross. Its genotype is:
exp: Production of recessive offspring proves that the tall parent carries t.

q: A tall plant produces only tall offspring when crossed with a dwarf plant. The most likely genotype of the tall plant is:
exp: TT × tt produces only Tt tall offspring.

q: The law of segregation states that:
opts: Alleles of different genes always assort together
Two alleles of a gene separate during gamete formation
exp: The two alleles remain discrete and segregate into different gametes.

q: Mendel's law of dominance explains why:
opts: Both alleles enter the same gamete
Only one of two contrasting traits appears in F1
Genes on the same chromosome assort independently
exp: In a heterozygote, the dominant allele expresses while the recessive allele is masked.

q: Which observation most directly supports the particulate nature of inheritance?
opts: The recessive trait reappears unchanged in F2
exp: Reappearance of the recessive trait shows that hereditary factors do not blend.

q: A heterozygous tall pea produces gametes in the ratio:
exp: Alleles segregate equally during gamete formation.

q: The probability of obtaining a dwarf offspring from Tt × Tt is:
exp: Only tt is dwarf, with probability 1/4.

q: The probability of a homozygous tall offspring from Tt × Tt is:
exp: TT occurs in one of four zygotic combinations.

q: Among tall F2 plants from Tt × Tt, the fraction that is heterozygous is:
exp: Tall genotypes are TT and Tt in a 1:2 ratio; 2 of 3 tall plants are Tt.

q: A back cross is a cross of an F1 hybrid with:
exp: Crossing F1 with either parental genotype is a back cross.

q: Every test cross is a back cross, but every back cross is not a test cross because:
opts: A test cross uses only the dominant parent
A test cross specifically uses the homozygous recessive parent
A back cross never uses a parent
exp: A back cross with the dominant parent is not a test cross.

q: In a dihybrid cross RrYy × RrYy, the F2 phenotypic ratio under independent assortment is:
exp: Independent assortment of two completely dominant gene pairs produces 9:3:3:1.

q: The number of different gamete types produced by RrYy is:
exp: The gametes are RY, Ry, rY and ry.

q: The number of gamete types produced by AaBbCc is:
exp: For n heterozygous independently assorting loci, gamete types = 2^n = 8.

q: The probability of aabb in AaBb × AaBb is:
exp: P(aa)=1/4 and P(bb)=1/4; multiply to obtain 1/16.

q: The probability of A_B_ offspring in AaBb × AaBb is:
exp: Dominant phenotype probability at each locus is 3/4; product is 9/16.

q: In a dihybrid test cross AaBb × aabb, independent assortment gives:
exp: The heterozygote forms four gamete types equally.

q: The law of independent assortment is best demonstrated by:
opts: A test cross involving one gene
exp: The 9:3:3:1 ratio reflects independent assortment of two gene pairs.

q: In AaBbCc × aabbcc, the probability of aabbcc is:
exp: The triple heterozygote produces abc gametes with probability 1/8.

q: How many distinct genotypes are possible in the offspring of AaBb × AaBb?
exp: Each locus has three genotypes, giving 3 × 3 = 9.

q: How many phenotypes occur in AaBb × AaBb with complete dominance and independent assortment?
exp: The four phenotypic classes are A_B_, A_bb, aaB_ and aabb.

q: The probability of an offspring heterozygous at both loci from AaBb × AaBb is:
exp: P(Aa)=1/2 and P(Bb)=1/2, so P(AaBb)=1/4.

q: If genes A and B assort independently, the probability of gamete Ab from AaBb is:
exp: Each allele choice has probability 1/2; their product is 1/4.

q: In incomplete dominance, the F1 phenotype is:
exp: Neither allele is completely dominant, so the heterozygote is intermediate.

q: In Snapdragon, red-flowered RR crossed with white rr produces F1 flowers that are:
exp: Rr has an intermediate pink phenotype.

q: The F2 phenotypic ratio in incomplete dominance is:
exp: Each genotype has a distinct phenotype, so phenotype and genotype ratios coincide.

q: Incomplete dominance does not violate the law of segregation because:
opts: The two alleles still separate during gamete formation
Only one allele enters all gametes
exp: Expression is intermediate, but alleles remain discrete and segregate.

q: Codominance differs from incomplete dominance because in codominance:
opts: Both alleles express fully in the heterozygote
exp: Both allelic products are simultaneously expressed.

q: The human AB blood group is an example of:
exp: IA and IB are both expressed in IAIB individuals.

q: The ABO blood group system is controlled by:
exp: IA, IB and i constitute multiple alleles, though each individual carries only two.

q: The genotype of a person with blood group O is:
exp: The O phenotype occurs only in homozygous ii individuals.

q: A person with blood group AB can produce gametes carrying:
exp: An IAIB individual segregates IA and IB.

q: Parents with blood groups A and B have a child with blood group O. The parental genotypes must be:
exp: An O child receives i from each parent.

q: A cross IAi × IBi can produce children with:
opts: A, B, AB and O groups
exp: The four genotypes IAIB, IAi, IBi and ii are equally possible.

q: An AB parent and an O parent can have offspring with blood groups:
exp: IAIB × ii gives IAi or IBi.

q: Multiple alleles means that:
opts: An individual possesses more than two alleles of a gene
More than two alternative alleles exist in a population
exp: A diploid individual still carries only two alleles at a locus.

q: Pleiotropy refers to:
opts: One trait controlled by many genes
One gene influencing more than one phenotypic trait
Two genes occupying the same locus
exp: A pleiotropic gene has multiple phenotypic effects.

q: Phenylketonuria illustrates pleiotropy because a single defective gene can affect:
opts: Several traits through altered phenylalanine metabolism
exp: The metabolic defect has multiple downstream phenotypic effects.

q: Polygenic inheritance involves:
opts: Many genes contributing additively to one trait
exp: Multiple loci contribute to continuous phenotypic variation.

q: Human skin colour is described in NCERT as an example of:
exp: Several gene pairs contribute additively to pigmentation.

q: If three independently assorting gene pairs contribute additively to skin colour, the number of phenotypic classes expected is:
exp: For n additive gene pairs, phenotypic classes = 2n+1 = 7.

q: With three additive gene pairs, the probability of the darkest phenotype in F2 is:
exp: The darkest phenotype requires all six contributing alleles; probability is (1/4)^3=1/64.

q: Continuous variation in a polygenic trait arises mainly because:
opts: Several genes have cumulative effects, often modified by environment
exp: Numerous additive genotypes create a graded distribution.

q: Which pair is correctly matched?
exp: Human skin colour is a standard polygenic example.

q: In incomplete dominance, a test cross of the F1 with the recessive parent gives:
exp: Rr × rr gives Rr and rr equally.

q: A pink Snapdragon crossed with another pink Snapdragon produces red, pink and white in the ratio:
exp: Rr × Rr gives RR:Rr:rr = 1:2:1.

q: Which statement about dominance is correct?
opts: Dominance means the dominant allele is more frequent
Dominance describes the phenotype of a heterozygote
exp: Dominance is a relationship between alleles in heterozygous condition.

q: A trait controlled by one gene but strongly influenced by environment is not necessarily:
exp: Environmental influence alone does not establish polygenic control.

q: The chromosomal theory of inheritance was proposed by:
exp: Sutton and Boveri related chromosome behaviour to Mendelian factors.

q: The parallel between genes and chromosomes includes that both:
opts: Exist in pairs in diploid cells and segregate during gamete formation
exp: Homologous chromosomes and alleles show paired occurrence and segregation.

q: Thomas Hunt Morgan used which organism extensively for genetic studies?
exp: Fruit flies have short generations and easily observed variations.

q: A major advantage of Drosophila for genetic experiments is:
opts: Many offspring and easily distinguishable sexes
exp: Drosophila breeds quickly and produces many progeny with visible traits.

q: Genes located on the same chromosome tend to be inherited together due to:
exp: Physical association on a chromosome causes linkage.

q: Recombination between linked genes results mainly from:
exp: Crossing over exchanges segments and creates recombinant allele combinations.

q: The frequency of recombination between two genes is generally proportional to:
opts: The distance between them on a chromosome
exp: Genes farther apart have a greater chance of a crossover between them.

q: One map unit is equivalent to:
exp: A centimorgan corresponds to one percent recombinant progeny.

q: If two genes show 12% recombination, their map distance is approximately:
exp: For short intervals, recombination percentage approximates map distance.

q: Complete linkage produces:
exp: Without crossing over, linked alleles remain in parental combinations.

q: In a test cross AB/ab × ab/ab with complete linkage, offspring are expected as:
exp: The heterozygote produces only AB and ab gametes.

q: In the same test cross, if genes assort independently, offspring ratio is:
exp: AB/ab produces four gamete classes equally when unlinked.

q: When linked dominant alleles are on one chromosome and recessive alleles on the homologous chromosome, the arrangement is:
exp: AB/ab is the coupling or cis arrangement.

q: The arrangement Ab/aB is called:
exp: Each homolog carries one dominant and one recessive allele.

q: Morgan observed that the proportion of parental combinations was greater than recombinants because:
opts: Genes were linked on the same chromosome
exp: Linkage preserves parental allele combinations.

q: Which statement about strongly linked genes is correct?
opts: They are usually close together and show low recombination
exp: Close genes are less likely to be separated by crossing over.

q: A recombination frequency of 50% usually indicates that genes are:
opts: Effectively unlinked or very far apart
exp: Independent assortment yields a maximum observable recombination of 50%.

q: The first genetic maps were prepared using recombination frequencies by:
exp: Sturtevant used recombination data to map Drosophila genes.

q: Crossing over occurs between:
exp: Exchange between non-sister chromatids creates recombinants.

q: The stage of meiosis most directly associated with crossing over is:
exp: Crossing over occurs during pachytene of prophase I.

q: Chiasmata become clearly visible during:
exp: Chiasmata are visible when homologues begin separating in diplotene.

q: If parental offspring are 420 and 430 while recombinants are 70 and 80, recombination frequency is:
exp: Recombinants=150 of 1000 total, so frequency=15%.

q: Two linked genes produce 18% recombinants in a test cross. The total parental percentage is:
exp: Parental classes together constitute 100-18=82%.

q: In a test cross with 20% recombination, each recombinant class is expected to constitute:
exp: The two reciprocal recombinant classes share 20% equally.

q: Linkage and recombination are best regarded as:
opts: Opposing consequences of gene location and crossing over
exp: Linkage preserves combinations, whereas crossing over generates new combinations.

q: In humans, females are homogametic because they produce:
exp: Human females are XX and all eggs carry X.

q: Human males are heterogametic because they produce:
exp: An XY male produces two types of sperm.

q: The sex of a human child is determined by:
opts: The type of sperm fertilising the ovum
exp: The egg always contributes X, while sperm contributes X or Y.

q: In humans, an XX zygote develops as:
exp: XX is the typical female chromosomal constitution.

q: In birds, the heterogametic sex is:
exp: Female birds produce Z- and W-bearing eggs.

q: In grasshoppers with XO type sex determination, males have:
opts: One X chromosome and no second sex chromosome
exp: Males are XO and females are XX.

q: In honey bees, males develop from:
exp: Arrhenotokous parthenogenesis produces haploid males.

q: A male honey bee has:
exp: Drones arise from unfertilised eggs and are haploid.

q: A pedigree is particularly useful for studying inheritance in humans because:
opts: Controlled crosses are not possible and family records can be analysed
exp: Pedigrees trace trait transmission through generations.

q: In a pedigree, a square generally represents:
exp: Squares denote males and circles denote females.

q: A filled symbol in a pedigree denotes:
exp: Shading indicates expression of the trait being studied.

q: Unaffected parents producing an affected child of either sex suggests most strongly:
exp: Both unaffected parents can be heterozygous carriers.

q: A trait appearing in every generation and affecting both sexes similarly is often:
exp: Autosomal dominant traits commonly show vertical transmission.

q: A Y-linked trait is transmitted from:
exp: Only sons inherit the father's Y chromosome.

q: An X-linked recessive trait is more frequent in males because males:
exp: A single recessive allele on the male X is expressed.

q: A haemophilic father and a normal homozygous mother will have:
opts: All carrier daughters and normal sons
exp: Daughters receive the affected paternal X and normal maternal X; sons receive paternal Y.

q: A carrier woman for haemophilia marries a normal man. The probability that a son is haemophilic is:
exp: Each son receives Y from father and has a 1/2 chance of receiving the affected maternal X.

q: For the same couple, the probability that a randomly born child is a haemophilic son is:
exp: Probability of male is 1/2 and affected maternal X is 1/2, giving 1/4.

q: A colour-blind man marries a homozygous normal woman. Their daughters will be:
exp: Each daughter receives the father's affected X and a normal maternal X.

q: A carrier woman for an X-linked recessive trait and an affected man can produce affected daughters with probability:
exp: An affected daughter must be female and receive the affected allele from the carrier mother: 1/2 × 1/2.

q: Father-to-son transmission rules out:
exp: A father gives his Y, not his X, to sons.

q: A trait transmitted by an affected mother to all children but by an affected father to none is suggestive of:
exp: Mitochondria are usually maternally inherited.

q: In an X-linked dominant trait, an affected father with a normal mother transmits the trait to:
exp: All daughters receive his affected X; sons receive his Y.

q: Consanguineous marriage increases the chance of:
exp: Relatives are more likely to carry the same rare recessive allele.

q: A normal woman whose father was haemophilic must be:
exp: She necessarily inherited the affected X from her father but is normal, so she is heterozygous.

q: A sudden heritable change in genetic material is called:
exp: Mutations generate new genetic variation.

q: Hugo de Vries proposed the mutation theory based largely on studies of:
exp: He studied evening primrose and emphasised sudden variations.

q: A point mutation involves:
opts: Change in the entire chromosome set
Change in a single base pair or a small DNA region
exp: Point mutations alter one nucleotide pair or a very small sequence.

q: Sickle-cell anaemia is caused by:
opts: A point mutation in the beta-globin gene
Failure of X chromosome segregation only
exp: A single base substitution changes glutamic acid to valine in beta-globin.

q: In sickle-cell anaemia, glutamic acid is replaced by valine at the:
exp: The classic substitution occurs at beta-globin position six.

q: Sickle-cell anaemia follows which inheritance pattern?
exp: The disease phenotype generally appears in homozygous HbS individuals.

q: A heterozygous individual HbA HbS is usually described as having:
exp: Heterozygotes usually carry the trait and may show symptoms under low oxygen.

q: Phenylketonuria is inherited as:
exp: It results from recessive mutation affecting phenylalanine metabolism.

q: Phenylketonuria involves impaired conversion of phenylalanine to:
exp: Deficient phenylalanine hydroxylase prevents normal tyrosine formation.

q: Thalassaemia is primarily a disorder of:
exp: Reduced synthesis of alpha or beta globin chains causes thalassaemia.

q: Thalassaemia differs from sickle-cell anaemia because thalassaemia is mainly a:
exp: Thalassaemia reduces the amount of a globin chain, whereas sickling changes beta-globin structure.

q: Haemophilia is commonly inherited as:
exp: The mutant clotting-factor allele is located on the X chromosome.

q: Colour blindness in humans is most commonly:
exp: Red-green colour blindness is a classic X-linked recessive trait.

q: Down syndrome is usually caused by:
exp: Nondisjunction can produce an extra chromosome 21.

q: The total chromosome number in a typical person with Down syndrome is:
exp: Trisomy 21 adds one chromosome to the normal diploid number.

q: Klinefelter syndrome usually has the karyotype:
exp: The affected male carries an additional X chromosome.

q: A common feature of Klinefelter syndrome is:
opts: Male with underdeveloped testes and possible gynaecomastia
exp: XXY individuals are male but often sterile with some feminised traits.

q: Turner syndrome usually has the karyotype:
exp: Turner syndrome results from monosomy of the X chromosome.

q: A typical person with Turner syndrome is:
exp: XO individuals develop as females with characteristic abnormalities.

q: Aneuploidy results from:
opts: Addition or loss of one or a few chromosomes
Duplication of the entire genome only
exp: Aneuploid cells have chromosome numbers not exact multiples of the haploid set.

q: Polyploidy means:
opts: Presence of more than two complete chromosome sets
exp: Polyploids contain additional complete sets of chromosomes.

q: Nondisjunction is the failure of:
opts: Chromosomes or chromatids to separate properly
exp: Improper segregation can generate aneuploid gametes.

q: A gamete with n+1 chromosomes fusing with a normal gamete produces a:
exp: The zygote has 2n+1 chromosomes.

q: A gamete with n-1 chromosomes fusing with a normal gamete produces a:
exp: The resulting chromosome number is 2n-1.

q: Which disorder is correctly matched with its chromosomal basis?
exp: Klinefelter syndrome is associated with 47,XXY.

q: Assertion: The recessive trait reappears in the F2 generation. Reason: The two alleles of a gene segregate during gamete formation.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Segregation allows recessive alleles to reunite in homozygous offspring.

q: Assertion: A test cross can distinguish TT from Tt. Reason: The tester is homozygous recessive and reveals the gametes of the dominant-phenotype parent.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Different gametes from TT and Tt produce different progeny patterns.

q: Assertion: Incomplete dominance violates Mendel's law of segregation. Reason: The F2 genotypic ratio remains 1:2:1.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The assertion is false; alleles still segregate normally.

q: Assertion: IA and IB are codominant. Reason: Both alleles produce their respective antigens in an AB individual.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Simultaneous antigen expression demonstrates codominance.

q: Assertion: An individual can carry three ABO alleles. Reason: The ABO locus has three alleles in the human population.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The population has three alleles, but a diploid individual carries only two.

q: Assertion: Linked genes may fail to show independent assortment. Reason: They are physically located on the same chromosome.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Physical association makes their inheritance non-independent.

q: Assertion: Recombination frequency can be used to estimate gene distance. Reason: Crossing over is generally more likely between genes farther apart.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Greater physical separation usually permits more crossovers.

q: Assertion: Recombination frequency between two genes can exceed 50%. Reason: Multiple crossovers are possible.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Multiple crossovers occur, but observable recombination does not exceed 50%.

q: Assertion: Human males are heterogametic. Reason: They form two types of sperm with respect to sex chromosomes.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Males produce X-bearing and Y-bearing sperm.

q: Assertion: In birds, the male determines the sex of offspring. Reason: Male birds are homogametic ZZ.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The reason is true, but heterogametic ZW females determine sex.

q: Assertion: X-linked recessive traits are more common in males. Reason: Males possess only one X chromosome.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: There is no second allele to mask a recessive mutation in males.

q: Assertion: A haemophilic father transmits haemophilia directly to his sons. Reason: Sons receive the Y chromosome from their father.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The reason is true and explains why direct father-to-son X-linked transmission does not occur.

q: Assertion: Consanguinity can increase autosomal recessive disease risk. Reason: Related individuals may share alleles inherited from a common ancestor.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Shared ancestry raises the chance both partners carry the same recessive allele.

q: Assertion: Sickle-cell anaemia is caused by a point mutation. Reason: A single base substitution changes one amino acid in beta-globin.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The substitution produces valine in place of glutamic acid.

q: Assertion: Thalassaemia is a quantitative haemoglobin defect. Reason: Synthesis of one globin chain is reduced or absent.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The amount of a globin chain is affected.

q: Assertion: Down syndrome is caused by monosomy 21. Reason: Nondisjunction can alter chromosome number.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Nondisjunction is relevant, but Down syndrome is trisomy 21.

q: Assertion: Turner syndrome occurs in phenotypic females. Reason: The typical karyotype is 45,XO.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: A single X chromosome supports female development but causes Turner features.

q: Assertion: Klinefelter syndrome individuals are genetically male. Reason: They usually possess a Y chromosome along with two X chromosomes.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Presence of Y initiates male development in 47,XXY individuals.

q: Assertion: Polygenic traits often show continuous variation. Reason: Multiple genes contribute cumulative effects to the phenotype.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: Many additive combinations create a spectrum of phenotypes.

q: Assertion: Pleiotropy and polygenic inheritance mean the same thing. Reason: Pleiotropy is one gene affecting many traits, whereas polygenic inheritance is many genes affecting one trait.
opts: Both Assertion and Reason are true, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are true, but Reason is not the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is true, but Reason is false
Assertion is false, but Reason is true
exp: The assertion is false while the reason correctly distinguishes them.

q: Match the inheritance pattern with the example: I. Incomplete dominance; II. Codominance; III. Polygenic inheritance; IV. Pleiotropy. a. ABO AB phenotype; b. Snapdragon flower colour; c. Human skin colour; d. Phenylketonuria
exp: Snapdragon, AB blood group, skin colour and phenylketonuria are the respective examples.

q: Match the cross with the expected ratio: I. Tt × Tt phenotype; II. Tt × tt phenotype; III. Rr × Rr in incomplete dominance; IV. AaBb × aabb with independent assortment. a. 1:1; b. 3:1; c. 1:2:1; d. 1:1:1:1
exp: The standard mono-, test-, incomplete-dominance and dihybrid-test-cross ratios match in that order.

q: Match the scientist with contribution: I. Mendel; II. Sutton and Boveri; III. Morgan; IV. Sturtevant. a. Gene mapping; b. Laws of inheritance; c. Chromosomal theory; d. Linkage studies in Drosophila
exp: These are the standard historical contributions described in NCERT.

q: Match the condition with karyotype/basis: I. Down syndrome; II. Turner syndrome; III. Klinefelter syndrome; IV. Sickle-cell anaemia. a. 45,XO; b. Point mutation in beta-globin; c. Trisomy 21; d. 47,XXY
exp: Each disorder is paired with its defining chromosomal or molecular basis.

q: Match the organism with sex-determination system: I. Humans; II. Birds; III. Grasshopper; IV. Honey bee. a. XO male; b. XY male; c. ZW female; d. Haplodiploidy
exp: Humans are XY, birds ZW, grasshoppers XO and honey bees haplodiploid.

q: Match the pedigree clue with likely inheritance: I. Father to all sons; II. Affected father to all daughters but no sons; III. Unaffected parents with affected child of either sex; IV. Affected mother to all children but affected father to none. a. Autosomal recessive; b. Y-linked; c. Mitochondrial; d. X-linked dominant
exp: These transmission patterns are diagnostic pedigree clues.

q: Match the term with definition: I. Linkage; II. Recombination; III. Centimorgan; IV. Nondisjunction. a. Failure of chromosome separation; b. Tendency of same-chromosome genes to be inherited together; c. New allele combinations; d. Unit corresponding to 1% recombination
exp: The terms match their standard genetic definitions.

q: Match the genotype with phenotype: I. IAIB; II. ii; III. HbAHbS; IV. HbSHbS. a. Sickle-cell trait; b. Blood group O; c. Blood group AB; d. Sickle-cell disease
exp: The genotype-phenotype associations are direct.

q: Match the meiotic event with stage: I. Synapsis; II. Crossing over; III. Chiasmata visible; IV. Homologous chromosomes separate. a. Diplotene; b. Pachytene; c. Anaphase I; d. Zygotene
exp: Synapsis, crossing over, visible chiasmata and homolog separation occur in zygotene, pachytene, diplotene and anaphase I.

q: Match the concept with numerical expression: I. Gamete types from n heterozygous loci; II. Phenotypic classes for n additive polygenes; III. Homozygous recessive at two loci in AaBb × AaBb; IV. Recombinant percentage. a. 2n+1; b. Recombinants/total ×100; c. 2^n; d. 1/16
exp: These formulas apply to independent heterozygous loci, additive classes, dihybrid probability and mapping.
